ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general beef noodle soup class (FOODON_00002473); no specific class exists for stir-fried pho variant. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for pho xao lan. A0FJP (noodle soup, beef-based) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific Vietnamese stir-fried beef noodle soup dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of pho stir-fried beef noodle soup is approximately 450 g, comprising rice noodles, stir-fried marinated beef slices, broth, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Pho Bo (Vietnamese Beef Noodle Soup) — adapted with stir-fry preparation adjustment; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Beef noodle soup (ID 173539) and rice noodles (ID 20066)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central; FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for pho xao lan exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for beef noodle soup (FDC ID 173539) with adjustments for stir-frying (higher fat from stir-fry oil, slightly lower water content) and marinated beef. FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Vietnamese pho bo used as a cross-reference. Per-100g values reflect a composite of approximately 40% rice noodles, 25% beef, 30% broth, and 5% vegetables and condiments.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Rice noodles, boiled (GI 53); Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pho xao lan. GI of 53 is taken from boiled rice noodles (GI 53, low category) as the primary glycemic driver per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables published in Diabetes Care. Stir-frying and protein/fat content from beef and sauce are expected to moderately lower the effective GI of the composite dish. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 60.75) / 100 ≈ 32.2, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 13.5 × 4.5 = 60.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1890 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load from soy sauce, oyster sauce, and beef broth; a single serving may provide approximately 82% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles (GL ~32 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in controlled portions, as the rice noodle base contributes a moderate-to-high glycemic load per serving (~32); pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables and reducing noodle quantity can help attenuate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and stir-fry oil, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious with this dish due to its saturated fat content (~1.4 g per 100 g) from beef and cooking oil, and its high sodium content which may elevate blood pressure; choosing leaner beef cuts and requesting reduced sauce can lower cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pho Stir-Fried Beef Noodle Soup (Phở Xào Lăn) is a Vietnamese-origin dish popular in Singapore, particularly at Vietnamese-Singaporean restaurants such as Co Chung. Unlike the classic simmered pho bo, this variant features flat rice noodles (banh pho) that are stir-fried in a wok over high heat together with thinly sliced beef that has been marinated in a blend of soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, and sesame oil. The dish is finished with a light beef broth poured over or served alongside, creating a hybrid between a dry stir-fry and a noodle soup. The texture is characterised by slightly charred, chewy rice noodles with tender, savory beef slices and a rich umami broth. Bean sprouts, spring onions, and fresh herbs are commonly added as garnishes. Nutritionally, a standard 450 g restaurant serving provides approximately 531 kcal, 32 g protein, 18 g fat, and 61 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~1890 mg per serving) due to the soy and oyster sauces and broth, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load is moderate-to-high (~32 per serving) driven by the rice noodle base, which is relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with shellfish or gluten allergies. It is a filling, protein-rich main course typically consumed at lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for pho xao lan exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy beef noodle soup data combined with stir-fry preparation adjustments and FAO/INFOODS SEA pho bo cross-reference. GI is estimated from the analogous boiled rice noodle GI value per Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated composites; no exact pho xao lan entry was found in any approved primary source. Values should be updated if HPB Singapore Foodo or a peer-reviewed SEA food composition study publishes a direct entry. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy beef noodle soup entry or FAO/INFOODS SEA reference used. 3. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source references used for this dish. 4. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on boiled rice noodles per Atkinson et al. 2021 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for pho xao lan was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Halal status is marked false as oyster sauce and soy sauce brands used at the specific restaurant are unverified for halal certification. 6. The dish is served at Co Chung Restaurant, Lorong Telok, Singapore, at SGD 24.50 per portion. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite data and are approximate.
